author: Ambrose of Milan
book: On the Duties of the Clergy.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Dogmatic Treatises, Ethical Works, and Sermons.
On the Duties of the Clergy.

Chapter IV. As it has been shown that he who injures another for the sake of his own advantage will undergo terrible punishment at the hand of his own conscience, it is referred that nothing is useful to one which is not in the same way useful to all. Thus there is no place among Christians for the question propounded by the philosophers about two shipwrecked persons, for they must show love and humility to all.

ccel.title: NPNF2-10. Ambrose: Selected Works and Letters

source.title: NPNF2-10. Ambrose: Selected Works and Letters

title: Chapter IV. As it has been shown that he who injures another for the sake of his own advantage will undergo terrible punishment at the hand of his own conscience, it is referred that nothing is useful to one which is not in the same way useful to all. Thus there is no place among Christians for the question propounded by the philosophers about two shipwrecked persons, for they must show love and humility to all.

source.title: NPNF2-10. Ambrose: Selected Works and Letters

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Hence we infer that a man who guides himself according to the ruling of nature, so as to be obedient to her, can never injure another.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: If he injures another, he violates nature, nor will he think that what he has gained is so much an advantage as a disadvantage.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: And what punishment is worse than the wounds of the conscience within?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What judgment harder than that of our hearts, whereby each one stands convicted and accuses himself of the injury that he has wrongfully done against his brother?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: This the Scriptures speak of very plainly, saying: “Out of the mouth of fools there is a rod for wrong-doing.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Folly, then, is condemned because it causes wrong-doing.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Ought we not rather to avoid this, than death, or loss, or want, or exile, or sickness?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Who would not think some blemish of body or loss of inheritance far less than some blemish of soul or loss of reputation?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is clear, then, that all must consider and hold that the advantage of the individual is the same as that of all, and that nothing must be considered advantageous except what is for the general good.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For how can one be benefited alone?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: That which is useless to all is harmful.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: I certainly cannot think that he who is useless to all can be of use to himself.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For if there is one law of nature for all, there is also one state of usefulness for all.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: And we are bound by the law of nature to act for the good of all.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is not, therefore, right for him who wishes the interests of another to be considered according to nature, to injure him against the law of nature.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For if those who run in a race are, as one hears, instructed and warned each one to win the race by swiftness of foot and not by any foul play, and to hasten on to victory by running as hard as they can, but not to dare to trip up another or push him aside with their hand, how much more in the course of this life ought the victory to be won by us, without falseness to another and cheating?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Some ask whether a wise man ought in case of a shipwreck to take away a plank from an ignorant sailor?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Although it seems better for the common good that a wise man rather than a fool should escape from shipwreck, yet I do not think that a Christian, a just and a wise man, ought to save his own life by the death of another; just as when he meets with an armed robber he cannot return his blows, lest in defending his life he should stain his love toward his neighbour.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: The verdict on this is plain and clear in the books of the Gospel.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: “Put up thy sword, for every one that taketh the sword shall perish with the sword.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What robber is more hateful than the persecutor who came to kill Christ?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But Christ would not be defended from the wounds of the persecutor, for He willed to heal all by His wounds.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Why dost thou consider thyself greater than another, when a Christian man ought to put others before himself, to claim nothing for himself, usurp no honours, claim no reward for his merits?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Why, next, art thou not wont to bear thy own troubles rather than to destroy another’s advantage?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For what is so contrary to nature as not to be content with what one has or to seek what is another’s, and to try to get it in shameful ways.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For if a virtuous life is in accordance with nature—for God made all things very good—then shameful living must be opposed to it.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: A virtuous and a shameful life cannot go together, since they are absolutely severed by the law of nature.
